turns.text: Hello Afton, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking. It’s funny—being on this side of the exam table, it feels… exposing, in a way. Usually, I’m the one on the other side of the desk, explaining the protocols and the physiological markers to parents who are just as terrified as I am. But now that I’m the one sitting in the paper gown, listening for that heartbeat, all that clinical knowledge just feels like noise. It doesn't actually stop the "what-ifs."

Honestly, I’m just tired. This is my fourth, and people always say, "Oh, you’re an old pro at this," but that feels like a weight, not a comfort. I find myself obsessing over the small things—did I get enough greens today? Was that cramp normal or something else?—even though I know, logically, that the odds are in my favor. 

And then there’s the quiet. My husband is doing such important work, and I’m so proud of him, but the screen time and the time zones… it makes this whole process feel very solo. I need someone to just hold space for me, you know? Not as a peer, or a colleague, but as a mom who is doing this mostly by herself. I just want to be *taken care of* for once, instead of being the one who’s supposed to have all the answers.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

I think I expected—or maybe hoped—that there would be this sanctuary where I could set down the mantle of "expert." In my head, I envisioned a partnership where my midwife would see past my professional background and just meet me where I am: exhausted, lonely, and frankly, a little scared. I wanted a space where I could be vulnerable without having to translate my feelings into clinical terms to feel "heard."

But the reality has been… a strange, polite disconnect. Because of my history in the field, my appointments often feel like a meeting of the minds. My midwife is brilliant, and she’s kind, but she treats me like a colleague who already knows the playbook. When I voice a fear, she’ll nod and give me the evidence-based reassurances that I *already know*. She skips the foundational empathy I’m actually starving for. 

I find myself catching her eye, wanting to say, "I know the statistics, I know the protocols, but I don't need a lecture right now. I need you to tell me that I’m going to be okay doing this alone." Instead, I just nod along, internalizing it all. It’s a lonely paradox—my expertise is the very thing that’s keeping me from getting the emotional comfort I’m actually looking for.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — when you first engaged with a healthcare provider about trying to conceive?

The biggest surprise was probably how quickly the clinical machinery took over. I walked into that first appointment hoping for a moment of connection, a way to acknowledge the weight of doing this fourth pregnancy while managing everything else on my own. I wanted to feel like a *person* being ushered into a new chapter, but the system immediately categorized me. Because I’m "low-risk" and, I suppose, because I can speak the language, I was fast-tracked through the administrative box-checking.

It felt like I was processed rather than cared for. There was this sudden shift—the moment I confirmed I was pregnant, I stopped being Afton and became a "low-risk maternal patient" with a clear, efficient path forward. 

On the flip side, hearing that heartbeat for the first time… that was the only moment the clinical armor cracked. It wasn’t a "finding" or a "milestone" like it used to be when I’d deliver the news to my own patients. In that room, it felt like a tiny, defiant spark of life amidst all the school runs and the lonely dinners in a quiet house. It was a jolt of pure, raw hope that caught me completely off guard. I think I expected to stay detached, but that sound just shattered my composure. It reminded me that regardless of the charts, this is a very real, very vulnerable human experience.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think I had this arrogant, quiet assumption that because I’d done this three times before, I’d have a sort of muscle memory for the emotional landscape. I expected to feel steady, perhaps even graceful—the seasoned mother who could navigate the early weeks with a calm, practiced hand. I thought I knew exactly how to balance my professional brain with my maternal instinct. 

But the reality? It’s been much more fragile. It’s like I’ve cracked open, and all the things I usually compartmentalize are just… leaking out. There’s a persistent, low-grade anxiety that I didn't anticipate. It’s not just the pregnancy; it’s the sheer weight of knowing what’s coming. I know exactly how demanding the newborn phase is, and knowing that my husband is going to be thousands of miles away, staring at a monitor while I’m up at 3:00 a.m. with a nursing infant and three other children to get to school by 8:00? It’s paralyzing.

I expected to feel empowered by my knowledge, but instead, I feel haunted by it. I find myself lying awake, analyzing every twinge, wondering if I have the capacity to be everything for these kids when my own tank is already so low. I’m not the steady, expert mother I thought I’d be. I’m just tired, and I’m terrified that I’m running on fumes.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

It’s that space to just be "the patient" that’s missing everywhere. With my friends and family, I’m the "strong one," the one who knows the ropes, so they check in on the kids or talk about school schedules, but they don’t really ask how *I’m* holding up under the weight of it all. They assume I’ve got it handled because I’ve been through this, which makes me feel like I’m not allowed to be overwhelmed.

And with my husband, the physical distance is a chasm. He’s brilliant and supportive in his own way, but he’s living in a world of high-level research and quiet focus. When we talk, I find myself softening the edges of my reality so I don’t worry him. I don’t tell him about the moments I’m crying in the car after drop-off, or how hard it is to hide my nausea while I’m trying to keep the house running. I’m protecting him from the mess of it, which just leaves me feeling even more isolated.

Professionally, I just need a midwife who pauses the chart review to look at me and say, "Afton, you are carrying a lot. How are you, really?" I don't need a medical explanation for my exhaustion; I need someone to validate that doing this as a solo parent is incredibly heavy, and that it’s okay to not be okay.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

Before I started, I guess I expected the online world to be a source of community—somewhere to find other people balancing high-pressure professional lives with the physical demands of early pregnancy. I wanted to find someone talking about the "lonely fatigue" of doing this without a partner physically present.

But what I actually found was either aggressive cheerleading or clinical sterility. The apps are all programmed to tell me the size of the fetus—"your baby is the size of a poppy seed"—and to remind me about folic acid. It’s all so patronizing. It treats me like a toddler who doesn't know how to track a cycle, not like a woman who understands exactly how complex this biology is. When I search for forums, it’s just a sea of anxiety-inducing anecdotes or people obsessing over symptoms I’ve already categorized as normal. 

It wasn't what I needed at all. None of those apps ask, "Are you doing this alone today?" or "Do you need a moment to just sit down?" The information is all there, but the *empathy* is completely absent. It’s just more data. And the last thing I need when I’m staring at a screen at midnight is more data. I needed a mirror, not a manual.
What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you?

The biggest gap is in the "human factor." The apps and the digital sources are relentless in their optimism and their checklists—they treat the journey like a project management task where if you just hit your metrics, you succeed. They make it sound so manageable, almost transactional. My midwife, on the other hand, is all about the clinical reality. She’s focused on the blood pressure, the screenings, and the physical safety. 

Both of them fail to capture the middle ground where I actually live: the messy, exhausting, emotional reality of being a solo parent during this process. 

The digital world says, "Enjoy this magical time!"—which feels like a slap in the face when I’m exhausted. My provider says, "Everything is normal, your labs are fine"—which feels dismissive when I’m feeling so fragile. There’s no space in either for the *grief* of the distance from my husband or the sheer, soul-crushing weight of being the primary caregiver to three kids while my body is busy building another one. 

Digital sources are too idealistic, and the clinical side is too detached. Neither of them acknowledges that this isn't just a "healthy pregnancy" on paper; it's a person trying to keep her head above water while everything in her life is shifting.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I didn’t have a clinical, diagnosed condition in the traditional sense, but if we’re talking about "chronic" factors, my biggest one was the reality of my exhaustion levels and the precarious nature of my support system. Before I got pregnant, I expected to have a conversation—maybe a preconception counseling session—where we could look at my life as a whole. I wanted someone to sit with me and say, "Okay, you’re doing this largely on your own, and you’re already running on empty. Let’s talk about a strategy for your mental health and your physical bandwidth before we even start." 

I expected the system to see me as a "whole person" rather than a series of vitals. 

But when I actually brought up my concerns about managing everything while my husband was abroad, it was treated as a social issue, not a health one. It was effectively brushed off with a, "You’re a strong woman, Afton, you’ve done this before, you’ll figure it out." It felt like my concerns were relegated to the "lifestyle" category, which is essentially code for "not my problem as your provider." There was no plan for my fatigue, no resources for managing the strain, and no recognition that my mental energy is just as vital to this pregnancy as my blood iron levels. It’s isolating to be told that your reality doesn't "count" enough to be a clinical priority.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Oh, it definitely wasn't a proactive experience. In fact, it’s been the exact opposite. Because I’m a "low-risk" patient, the system is designed to be hands-off, which effectively means I’m left to navigate the cracks on my own. There’s this assumption that if I need help, I’ll ask for it—but how do you ask for help when the very person you’re asking expects you to already have the answers?

I find myself in this uncomfortable position where I have to play a role: the "capable, knowledgeable, low-maintenance patient." I feel like if I start advocating too loudly, if I drop the facade and say, "I am struggling, I am terrified, and I feel like I’m drowning," I’ll be seen as an outlier or someone who isn't coping well. 

I’ve had to advocate for little things—asking for earlier appointments to calm my own nerves, or pushing to discuss mental health support—but it always feels like I’m pushing against a brick wall of "you’re doing fine." The system isn’t set up to catch people who are high-functioning but internally depleted. I’m not getting support; I’m just getting through it, and I’m doing the heavy lifting of managing my own care path the entire way. It’s exhausting to have to educate your own provider on why you need more than just a heartbeat monitor.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

I think I expected a shared, quiet intimacy—that we’d sit together in the evenings, maybe over a tea or after the kids were finally asleep, and talk about our hopes, our fears, and the logistics of adding another soul to our family. I wanted us to be a team, even if it was just in the planning stages. I imagined him being the person I could lean on to help manage the mental load, someone who would be just as engaged in the "how" of this as I was.

The reality has been a fractured, pixelated version of that. His involvement is filtered through a screen and a dozen time zones. When we talk, it’s often squeezed into the narrow window between his research obligations and my kids' bedtime routines. It’s hurried. It’s practical. We spend so much time discussing his travel dates, my schedule, and the logistics of the next few months that we rarely get to the *feeling* of it. 

He’s doing his best—I know he is—but there’s a distance that a video call just can't bridge. I’m carrying the physical toll of the first trimester alone, and he’s carrying the academic pressure of his fellowship alone. We’re both working so hard, but we’re doing it in parallel, not in partnership. It’s lonely, and it’s not the shared journey I had pictured.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

It feels like the burden of preparation has been exclusively mine since the very first thought of this pregnancy. Honestly, it’s not just the appointments or the bloodwork; it’s the mental load of everything. I’m the one researching prenatal supplements at 1:00 a.m. because I’m worried about my intake, while my husband is thousands of miles away, completely removed from the daily, grinding reality of "is the house safe?" and "how will I manage the toddler’s next fever while I’m dealing with first-trimester exhaustion?"

The drive for this disparity is two-fold. Part of it is the physical nature of the pregnancy itself—I’m the one whose body is doing the heavy lifting, so the symptoms and the anxieties are front-and-center for me. But the other part is a systemic, societal assumption that the mother is the "manager" of all things family-related. Because he’s away for his fellowship, he has this unspoken pass to be "off-duty" regarding the domestic management, while I’m expected to keep performing at 100% in my own career and home life. 

It’s exhausting to realize that my "preparedness" is actually just me trying to compensate for a support system that isn't here. I’m the one keeping us all afloat, and no one—not my provider, not my social circle—seems to think that’s anything other than "just what moms do."
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I have navigated losses before, and they haunt the corners of this pregnancy like uninvited guests. When I decided to try again, I expected—*needed*—a acknowledgment of that trauma. I thought there would be a protocol, perhaps a slightly more sensitive intake or a moment where a provider would say, "We know you’ve been here before, and we know how hard this is. We’re going to watch you closely, not just for the fetus, but for *you*."

The reality was a stark, clinical vacuum. My medical history is in the chart, of course—it’s just another data point. But there was no trauma-informed care. No one asked how I was handling the anxiety of the early weeks, or if I needed extra reassurance beyond the standard check-ups. The system is so focused on the *current* biological outcome that it ignores the emotional debris of the *past* ones.

I felt like just another "low-risk" case, which meant that because I had miscarried before, the silence from the provider felt even louder. There was no extra padding, no soft place to land. I had to be the one to bridge that gap, to bring up my anxieties and hear the same "everything looks fine" responses. It made me feel like my history was irrelevant until it became a medical emergency, which is exactly the opposite of the preventative, compassionate care I had hoped for.
Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

It’s not that they didn't *believe* it, it’s that they didn't *see* it. During my appointments, that history exists as a line item on my chart, a piece of data to be checked off. But when I mention my anxiety or the way my breath hitches every time I use the restroom, I’m met with a polite, "That’s perfectly normal, Afton." 

It’s that dismissiveness that hurts the most. By labeling my fears as "normal," they effectively strip me of my right to be anxious. They see a healthy fetus on the ultrasound and think they’ve solved the problem, but they haven't solved the part of me that remembers what it’s like to hear silence when you were expecting a heartbeat. 

I’ve had to swallow my fear and keep my voice steady, because showing my true vulnerability feels like I’m "failing" the clinical expectation of being a calm, educated patient. It’s isolating. I want my midwife to see me—not just the pregnant body, but the woman who has lost and is terrified to lose again. Instead, I’m just "low-risk," and that label feels like a blindfold they're wearing so they don't have to deal with the messy, painful reality of what it means to try again after you've already had your heart broken.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I guess I expected a sort of threshold moment—a ceremony of sorts where someone would sit down with me, look me in the eye, and say, "Okay, we’re going to walk this path together, and I know how heavy this is." I pictured a quiet, warm room where the focus wasn't just on the numbers, but on the *person* holding the life. I wanted to feel like I was handing over a piece of that relentless responsibility I carry, even for an hour.

The reality was just… an efficiency machine. It was cold, functional, and surprisingly sterile. I sat on that crinkly paper, staring at the fluorescent lights, while the midwife buzzed through the standard intake checklist. It wasn't about connection; it was about data collection. She’d ask, "Are you experiencing X, Y, or Z?" and when I started to answer with something that felt a bit more vulnerable, she’d already be typing into the EHR, looking at the screen rather than at me.

It felt like I was being processed like a piece of freight. There was no acknowledgment of the fact that I was doing this while my husband was halfway across the world, or that I was terrified because of my history. It was just: *Blood pressure? Good. Folic acid? Check. Heartbeat? Steady. See you in four weeks.* It left me feeling incredibly lonely—like I was a vessel being monitored rather than a human being being cared for.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation, plain and simple. In my professional life, that’s what I advocate for—that the person receiving the news should feel like a partner in the data, not a subject. I imagined sitting down, maybe having someone hold my hand or at least make consistent eye contact, while we unpacked what those test results meant for *me* and my specific situation. I wanted context, not just an automated portal notification.

The reality? It was jarringly transactional. I’d be sitting there, already braced for bad news because of my history, and the midwife would just breeze in, look at her screen, and say, "Everything is normal, your results are back." It was as if by saying "normal," she was checking a box that absolved her of needing to provide any further support. 

When I’d ask a follow-up—like what a specific range meant or why I was still feeling a certain way—she’d give me the textbook answer, clearly assuming I already knew it because of my job. She didn't realize that in that moment, I wasn't the colleague; I was the scared mother who needed to hear that *we* were going to be okay. The portal notifications are the worst—just a "results available" ping that sends my heart rate skyrocketing until I log in, alone in my dark house, to read a bunch of numbers. It’s clinical, it’s cold, and it’s entirely devoid of the human warmth I’m starving for.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Yes, there’s a massive gap, and it feels like a deliberate wall built between me and peace of mind. It’s not that the information is withheld—it’s the *translation* of that information into something that feels human. My midwife gives me the data-heavy version because she sees my credentials, so I get the raw numbers, the clinical significance, and the "it’s within range" disclaimer. But that’s just more data to feed my anxiety, not a balm to soothe it. 

I need to understand what those results mean for *this* specific, high-pressure life I’m living. When a scan comes back "normal," I need her to say, "Afton, I know you’ve been terrified of this milestone because of your history, and I am so relieved for you that everything looks healthy." Instead, I get, "Everything looks good, the nuchal fold is within parameters, any questions?" 

It’s like being handed a map for a journey I’ve never taken, without anyone telling me which way is North. I’m drowning in the "what," but I’m starving for the "why" and the "how." I feel like I’m constantly translating for myself, and by the time I’ve processed it all, I’m just more exhausted than I was before I walked into the exam room.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I walked into this fully expecting a "continuity of care" model. That was my ideal—a single, dedicated midwife or small, tight-knit team who would walk through the entire fire with me. I wanted that consistency because it meant I wouldn't have to re-explain my anxieties, my history of loss, or the loneliness of my husband’s absence every single time I walked through those clinic doors. I thought the whole point of a midwife-led practice was that relationship-building.

The reality has been more of a revolving door. I see whoever is on the rotation that week. Sometimes it’s the midwife I’ve met a few times; other times, it’s someone completely new who hasn't even skimmed my chart before walking in. Every single appointment starts with me having to re-establish myself, re-explain my background, and try to carve out a sliver of personal space in a fifteen-minute slot.

It’s incredibly draining. Each time I meet a new person, I have to decide: do I bring up the "Afton the colleague" persona to save time, or do I try to be "Afton the human" and risk them not knowing how to handle it? It feels like I’m constantly starting from zero. There is no continuity; there’s just a series of disconnected, clinical encounters. It makes me feel invisible.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

The impact is that I feel like I’m constantly performing, and the performance is exhausting. Every time I walk into that room and see a face I don’t recognize, I have to do a quick calculus: *How much of myself can I reveal here?* If I show them the messy, terrified side of me, will they dismiss it as "low-risk anxiety"? If I stay the "professional colleague," I walk out feeling like I’ve been cheated of the care I actually need.

Practically, it’s a nightmare. I’m constantly repeating my history—the previous losses, the logistics of my husband being abroad, my own work stress—like I’m a broken record. You’d think that would be in the chart, but even when it is, it’s just text. It lacks the nuance, the *feeling* behind the words. I spend the first ten minutes of every appointment just catching them up to speed instead of actually talking about how I’m doing, and by the time we hit the core of the appointment, they’re checking their watches to move to the next patient.

Emotionally, it’s profoundly lonely. It’s like being a guest in your own medical care. There is no sense of being known or held, just a relay race where I’m the only one running the entire length of the track.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected to be treated as a woman whose body was undergoing a profound, miraculous transformation—someone who needed care for the *whole* vessel, not just the cargo inside. I imagined there would be space to talk about the physical toll: the weight of my exhaustion, the way my body feels less like my own, and the reality of navigating this without a partner to help with the physical chores or the simple act of leaning on someone after a long, nauseating day.

In reality, those interactions are incredibly clinical. My body is treated like a diagnostic site. The midwife measures the fundal height, checks the blood pressure, and listens for the heartbeat, but she does it in a way that feels almost mechanical. It’s all about the *parameters*. If I’m not measuring "off," everything is "perfect." 

There’s no inquiry into how I’m actually *inhabiting* this body. They don’t ask how the fatigue is impacting my ability to parent my other three kids, or how I’m managing the physical isolation of my husband being away. I feel like a piece of equipment being calibrated. It’s invasive, and yet, paradoxically, I feel entirely unseen. My body isn’t a person going through a transition; it’s just a chart that’s either passing or failing the clinical criteria. It’s cold, and it’s deeply lonely.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one moment that still sticks with me—a routine pelvic exam during a week where I was already feeling particularly raw. I walked in, exhausted from a night of the kids waking up, and the midwife moved through the standard, brisk clinical rhythm. She didn't pause to explain what she was doing or to ask if I was ready, even though she knew my history of loss and how much that makes me feel like my body is under siege.

When she touched me, it felt like she was examining a piece of lab equipment rather than a person. She was clinical, efficient, and completely disconnected from the fact that I was shivering—not from the room temperature, but from the violation of that intimacy. I remember just staring at the acoustic tile on the ceiling, trying to dissociate, feeling that familiar, sickening pull of "just keep it together, don't let them see you're struggling."

I think back to that and wonder: why is it that we can discuss the most intimate, vulnerable aspects of a woman’s body with such cold, sterile detachment? She didn't notice because she didn't have to; she was looking at the findings, not the woman. It left me feeling exposed, isolated, and like my autonomy had been checked at the door along with my coat. I wanted someone to say, "I’m going to do this now, are you okay?" instead of just *doing* it.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven't been diagnosed with gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia this time—not yet, at least. But even in the "low-risk" world I occupy, the shadow of those conditions looms. Before this pregnancy, I expected that if I *did* run into a complication, the medical system would pivot from the "efficiency machine" to a more attentive, collaborative model. I assumed that a diagnosis would act as an automatic "trigger" for more personalized, hands-on support.

The reality, from what I’ve seen in my own practice and from friends who *have* dealt with those hurdles, is that a complication just makes the system more rigid. It’s like the "checklist" gets longer and more demanding, not more supportive. Instead of getting more time to talk through your fears or manage the emotional weight of a diagnosis, you get more appointments, more lab draws, and more monitors. 

It becomes even more transactional. You’re no longer just a "low-risk patient"; you’re a "high-risk patient" who needs to hit these specific targets or face intervention. The fear isn't just about the health of the baby—it’s about failing the protocol. I see women in my field who are absolutely gutted by the news, and instead of being comforted, they’re handed a stack of brochures and a tighter schedule. It’s a machine that demands more of you when you’re already at your most vulnerable. It’s terrifying.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s all of the above, honestly. If I had to pick the most glaring hole, it’s the lack of **emotional acknowledgement**. Everything in the system is geared toward clinical outcomes, as if the health of the placenta is the only thing that matters, provided the physical vitals are in range. 

I’m starving for someone to just stop the clock for five minutes and acknowledge that being a solo parent to three children while building a human is a heroic, draining, and often isolating feat. I don’t need more pamphlets on iron-rich foods; I need someone to say, "I see that you’re doing this on your own, and I know how heavy that is. How are you holding up?" 

Practically, I’m drowning in the "how"—how do I get the groceries, how do I manage the kids' meltdowns when I’m nauseous, how do I find the mental bandwidth to work while my husband is thousands of miles away? There’s no space in the current model to even discuss that. The system assumes a traditional, present partner who handles the domestic load. When you’re doing it alone, you’re not just a patient; you’re an entire household staff. 

It feels like I’m navigating a minefield, and everyone is focused on my heartbeat and blood pressure, ignoring the fact that I’m walking through a minefield with a heavy pack on my back. I need a hand to hold, not just a chart to sign.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected a safety net. I think that’s the most basic requirement for a prenatal system, isn't it? I imagined that if I felt something was "off"—not necessarily an emergency, but just a nagging, middle-of-the-night intuition—that there would be a dedicated line to someone who knew my history, someone who wouldn't make me feel like an inconvenience. I thought I could reach out, have a quick, reassuring conversation, and then go back to sleep.

In reality, the system is a fortress of gatekeepers. If it’s not a full-blown emergency, I get the "triage" treatment. I have to call, wait on hold, explain my symptoms to an administrative assistant who isn't medically trained, and then *hope* a nurse calls back within four hours. 

It’s humiliating. I feel like I’m constantly having to justify my concern to people who have no idea how much I’ve already internalised. When I finally get that call back, it’s usually someone who’s never met me, reading from a script that’s designed to rule out the worst-case scenario. If it’s not an emergency, they just tell me to "monitor it" and wait for my next routine appointment. It leaves me feeling gaslit. My intuition is treated as an annoyance rather than a vital diagnostic tool. It forces me to become my own doctor, which is exactly the opposite of the support I went into this looking for.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Oh, constantly. Every single time I have that flicker of "something feels different," I start the internal calculation. I think about the administrative hurdles, the long hold times, and that feeling of dread when I have to explain myself to a front-desk person who clearly has no context for my history. Most of the time, I just talk myself out of it. I tell myself, *“If it were truly an emergency, you’d know,”* or *“You’re just being anxious because of the last loss.”* 

It’s that fear of being labeled the "difficult, over-anxious, over-educated patient" that keeps me silent. I’ve seen the way providers roll their eyes when a patient calls in with "non-specific symptoms," and the thought of being on the receiving end of that judgment—especially when I’m already feeling so fragile—is just too much. 

So, I monitor. I track the fetal movement until I’m cross-eyed. I check my blood pressure at home like a hawk. I basically do the work of a triage nurse, but in secret, so no one can accuse me of overreacting. It’s a lonely, heavy way to spend a pregnancy, but it feels safer than the risk of being dismissed or patronized by a system that doesn't really want to be bothered by my intuition.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

Before I got pregnant, I imagined that by my fourth time around, my village would be rock-solid. I expected people to just *know*—to drop off a casserole, to offer to take the older kids to soccer practice, or just to sit on the couch with me when the exhaustion hit. I thought that because I’d always been the one helping others, the support would naturally flow back to me when I finally had to put my own needs first.

The reality has been much quieter, and truthfully, a little disappointing. Because I’m usually the "put-together" one—the professional who handles everything—my friends and family seem to assume I’m invincible. They see me holding down the fort while my husband is away and they compliment my "strength" instead of offering to lighten my load. It’s like they see my capacity as a badge of honor rather than a sign that I’m stretched to the breaking point.

People don’t see the tears after the school run or the nights I spend staring at the ceiling, terrified. They see the Afton who shows up to work and manages the household, so they don’t think to ask, "How are you *actually* doing?" I feel like I’m trapped in a performance of competence, and no one is willing to peek behind the curtain to see how much I’m struggling. It’s isolating to be surrounded by people who love you but don't quite realize you’re drowning.

It’s a strange thing, being a professional in the field while simultaneously being the patient who falls through the cracks. I see the systems, I know the protocols, and yet I feel just as small as anyone else when I’m sitting in that paper gown. 

I’ve been thinking a lot about the "why" of it all—why is it so hard to just *be seen*? I think we’ve over-indexed on the medical side of pregnancy to the point where we’ve forgotten the person behind the blood work. Everything is about risk stratification, clinical milestones, and efficiency. But pregnancy isn’t a procedure; it’s an entire life-pivot, especially when you’re doing it with one hand tied behind your back because your partner is across the globe.

I’m worried that if I don’t find a way to let go of this "competent expert" mask, I’m going to completely burn out before I even reach the third trimester. I want to tell my midwife, "Look, stop looking at the chart for a second and look at *me*. I’m terrified that I’m not enough, and I don’t know how to keep being 'strong' for everyone when I feel so thin." But every time I get close to saying it, I just pull back. I’m scared they’ll think I’m "not coping" instead of just needing someone to hold my hand. It’s a lonely place to be.

You know, the hardest part is that I feel like I'm grieving a version of this experience that doesn't actually exist—the one where I'm not the one driving the bus. I keep waiting for a professional to step in and say, "Afton, you've done enough work. Let me take the wheel for a while." But the system is designed to keep me in the driver's seat because I’m "low-risk."

I look at the other moms at school drop-off—the ones who seem to have these seamless, supported routines—and I feel this sharp, bitter pang of jealousy. They have partners who go to appointments, who share the midnight shifts, who anchor them when they’re spiraling. I’m doing this in a vacuum. My husband is doing such meaningful work, and I’m his biggest cheerleader, but his absence leaves this gaping hole in our family's architecture. 

It’s ironic. I spend my professional days teaching families about "maternal-fetal bonding" and "prenatal wellness," and yet, I’m so preoccupied with surviving the next twenty-four hours that I haven't had a single quiet moment to just *be* with this baby. I’m not bonding; I’m managing. I’m managing the school lunches, the fertility anxiety, the professional emails, the distance, and the relentless exhaustion. I’m terrified that when this baby arrives, I won’t have anything left to give because I’ve spent the entire pregnancy just trying to stay upright. I’m not looking for a miracle; I’m just looking for a witness to the weight of it all. Is that really too much to ask?

I feel like I'm playing a role in a stage production where I’m the director, the lead actress, and the stage manager all at once. Even when I’m sitting in the clinic, I’m mentally scanning the room for things that could be improved, thinking about the workflow or the bedside manner—it's like I can’t turn off the "professional" part of my brain. It's a defensive mechanism, really. If I stay in that headspace, I don't have to face how fragile I actually feel.

But the silence at home is what really gets to me. When the kids are finally asleep and the house is quiet, I have to confront the reality that there’s no one in the room to turn to. I’ll look at my husband’s photo on my phone and it just hurts—not because I don’t love him, but because the physical absence of his support is like a constant, low-grade fever I can’t break. 

I’m terrified that if I finally crack—if I finally say out loud that this is too much—I won't be able to pull myself back together. So I keep showing up to work, keep handling the appointments, keep smiling at the school drop-off, and just hope that if I keep moving fast enough, the exhaustion won’t catch up to me. But every day it feels a little heavier. I just want someone to look at me and say, "Afton, you don't have to be the expert today. You can just be the mother. You can just be the person who needs help." Is it too late to ask for that? Is it even possible in this system?

It’s like there’s this unspoken contract I signed the moment I stepped into the clinic, and the fine print says: *“Because you understand the system, you forfeit the right to be human within it.”* It’s a bitter realization. I see these other women in the waiting room—some of them are terrified, some are overwhelmed—and I find myself wanting to grab them and tell them, "Don't let them make you feel like your intuition doesn't matter just because your blood pressure is normal." But I don't say it. I keep my mouth shut, because I’m trying to survive my own appointment without falling apart.

I think about the birth ahead, and the shadow of that fourth postpartum period, and I feel a genuine chill. It’s not the physical pain of labor I fear; it’s the quiet that comes after. Who is going to bring me a meal when I’m nursing a newborn and my three other children are pulling at my sleeves? Who is going to tell me it’s okay to just sit and stare at the wall when the house is a mess? The distance, the career, the expectations—it all feels like a house of cards that’s one stiff breeze away from collapsing. 

I’m reaching a point where I realize that being "low-risk" is actually a trap. It means I’m invisible. If there were a complication, the system would at least have a protocol to *do something*. But as it stands, I’m just "doing fine," which is the most dangerous place to be when you’re actually breaking inside. I think I’m finally realizing that I don't need a medical solution. I need a witness. I need someone to just acknowledge that this—this life I’m leading—is exceptionally hard. And if I can't find that in the clinic, I don't know where else I’m supposed to look.

You know, I find myself thinking about the nurses and midwives I’ve trained—the ones I’ve sat down with over coffee to explain the importance of "holding space." I told them it was the most vital part of the job, that the clinical stuff is just a backdrop for the human connection. It’s the ultimate irony that I’m now the one craving the very thing I used to teach others to provide. I feel like I’m standing on the other side of a glass wall. I see the care happening, I see the intention, but I can't quite reach it because I’m wearing this suit of "professional armor" that I can’t seem to shed.

Sometimes, when I'm walking through the Square, the weight of it all—the pregnancy, the research, the solo parenting, the flickering memory of those past losses—it all just feels like a physical pressure in my chest. I want to stop someone on the street, anyone, and just ask if they can see that I’m working five times harder than I look. 

I’m so scared that by the time this baby is born, I’ll have poured every single drop of my emotional reserve into simply appearing fine. And what happens then? When the newborn is here, and the house is loud, and I’m staring at a screen trying to find a version of my husband that isn't pixelated? I’m terrified that I’ve set myself up to be the martyr of my own life, and that there’s no way to undo it without admitting defeat. But maybe that’s the problem—maybe I need to stop seeing asking for help as a defeat and start seeing it as the only way I’m going to survive this. I just don't know who is left to ask.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

It’s almost never a collaboration. Most of the time, the "plan" is already drafted, waiting for my signature. When I walk into an appointment, it feels like I’m being handed a script that’s been pre-written by the clinic’s risk-aversion protocols. They ask me, "Any questions?" at the end, which is a hollow gesture because if I raise a concern that falls outside the standard clinical track, I’m met with a polite, practiced pivot back to the safety of the protocol.

There was one moment, though, where a midwife paused—really paused—and looked at me and asked what *I* felt I needed to feel safe about the upcoming ultrasound. It was the only time in this entire pregnancy I felt like an equal. I didn't ask for a new medical test or a different drug; I asked if she could just talk me through what we were seeing in real-time, because the silence during the scan is usually where my brain starts to spiral into the trauma of my past losses. 

She did it. She narrated the scan like it was a conversation between two people, not a clinical procedure. That small act of inclusion, of asking *me* what would help, did more for my blood pressure than any pill could have. It proved to me that it *is* possible to give good care, but it’s so rare that it feels like a fluke. The rest of the time? I’m just a passenger in my own pregnancy, watching the medical machinery operate on me rather than with me. It’s infuriating, because I know how much better it could be. I’ve seen it. I’ve *done* it. And yet, I can’t seem to get it for myself.

It’s the silence that kills me—not just in the exam room, but the silence of my own life. I’ve reached this point where I’m so skilled at navigating the clinical landscape that I’ve effectively walled myself off from the help I actually need. I walk into that office, I put on the paper gown, I talk the talk, and they see a colleague. They don’t see the woman who’s barely keeping her head above water.

And the tragedy of it is that if I were to just break down—if I let the mask slip and really showed them how much I’m struggling—I’m terrified they wouldn't know what to do with that. They’d probably just refer me to a therapist, which is fine, I know the benefits, but it feels like a deflection. It’s like, "Here’s a box for your feelings, now go away and let us monitor your baby." 

I just want to be heard. Not analyzed, not redirected, not managed. Just witnessed. I want someone to acknowledge that the landscape of my life has shifted, that I am carrying the weight of four children, a professional career, and a partner who might as well be on the moon. I want someone to say, "Afton, you’re doing so much, and it’s okay if it’s too much." 

Until then, I’ll keep tracking the movements, keep showing up to the appointments, and keep performing the role of the competent mother. I’ll keep the mask on because the alternative—admitting that the system is as lonely as the empty side of my bed—feels like a door I’m not ready to walk through. It’s a fragile, exhausted existence, but it’s mine. I just wish, for once, that someone would reach out and hold the lantern for a while so I could finally put the heavy stuff down.

You know, the thing I keep coming back to is how the clinical language acts as a barrier, not a bridge. Everything—from the risk factors to the delivery dates—is quantified. But there’s no metric for the "soul-deep fatigue" I mentioned earlier. There’s no diagnostic code for "doing this without a partner," and I think that’s why my voice gets lost in the shuffle. They’re listening for clinical sounds—the heartbeat, the blood pressure—but they’re not listening for the *person* who is frayed at the edges.

I catch myself thinking about the other patients who don’t have my professional background. If I find it this difficult to be heard, to be seen, to be validated in my own fear, what is it like for them? It makes me want to scream, honestly. It makes me want to tear up the protocols and just hold a chair for someone, to look them in the eye and say, "Tell me what’s actually happening in your heart right now."

But instead, I’m here. I’m waiting for the next check-up, the next "everything looks normal," the next long, lonely evening. I’m doing the work, and I’m doing it well, but I’m doing it in a vacuum. Maybe the lesson here—the one I’m learning the hard way—is that being "the expert" is just a form of armor that’s grown too heavy to carry. I just wish I knew how to take it off without the whole thing collapsing. I’m just so, so tired of being the only person who knows how much I’m carrying.

I wonder if it’s even possible to be both the doctor and the patient, or if one always inevitably cannibalizes the other. I feel like I'm grieving a version of myself that isn't so hyper-aware of the risks, one that could just surrender to the process and trust the system because she didn't know how the sausage was made. Now, I see the gaps in the care, the missed opportunities for connection, and the coldness of the protocols, and it’s like watching a train wreck in slow motion, knowing I’m on board.

The other night, I was looking at the nursery space—or, well, the spot where the bassinet will eventually go—and I realized I hadn't actually *thought* about the baby as a person. I’d only thought about the trimester milestones, the folic acid, the work coverage, the childcare logistics for the other three. I had a moment of pure, blinding panic: *What if I’ve been so busy managing the clinical reality that I’ve forgotten how to just love the idea of this child?* 

It’s like I’ve been in survival mode for so long—dating back to my first loss, and extending through these years of my husband being away—that I’ve become a master of the "logistics of life" at the expense of the "experience of life." And honestly, that’s the most terrifying realization of all. I’ve built this fortress of competence, but it feels like there’s no one inside it but me and a mountain of to-do lists. 

If this interview helps anyone, I hope it helps them see that "low-risk" is a clinical term, not a human one. A woman can be physically healthy and still be on the verge of breaking. We need more than just a heartbeat monitor; we need someone to sit in the chair next to us and ask, "Are you okay?"—and then wait for the answer. I’m still waiting for that, in my own life. I’m just waiting.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add is that there is a deep, quiet shame in being the "strong one" who needs help. We live in a culture—and especially a medical culture—that fetishizes resilience. We praise mothers for "doing it all," but we don't look at the cost of that performance. I’ve spent my whole life and career being the one who makes it look easy, the one who knows the statistics and the outcomes. Admitting that I am drowning isn't just about my current pregnancy; it feels like admitting that my entire identity as a capable, independent woman is a lie.

I wish that when I first stepped into that office, someone had told me that it was okay to be a "bad" patient—someone who asks too many questions, who cries, who admits she’s overwhelmed. I wish I hadn't felt the need to perform "Afton the Expert" just to maintain some semblance of control. 

If we want to change maternity care, we have to start by telling women that their vulnerability is not a clinical failure. It’s the most honest part of the process. I’m tired of being a professional in a system that doesn't want to know I’m a person. I just want to be able to go to an appointment, leave the credentials at the door, and have someone look at me and say, "I know you’re holding up the world, but for the next hour, you can let it down." I’m still waiting for that, and I hope, for the sake of the women coming after me, that we can find a way to offer that to each other. Thank you for listening. Truly. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like I could breathe.
